framework.cuda_min_version: 11.8 (optional, for GPU signature computation and XGBoost)
framework.reasoning: The classifier itself is XGBoost, not a neural network -- but path simulation and signature computation benefit from vectorized tensor ops, and the paper's own pipeline is GPU-accelerated (RTX 3080 Ti). We use PyTorch tensors for simulating batches of paths and computing signatures (via a simple iterated-integral recursion), then hand off the resulting feature matrix to XGBoost (CPU or GPU histogram method) for classification. A small optional PyTorch MLP reproduces the paper's Section 6.7 neural-network baseline.

module_hierarchy.description: XGBoost multi-class classifier wrapper with the paper's exact hyperparameters.
PyTorch MLP robustness-check baseline (Section 6.7): 128-64-32 hidden layers, batch norm, dropout.
Built-in XGBoost gain importance + permutation importance side by side.
Builds the labelled (signature, class) dataset for each of the paper's 9 named experiments (5.1-5.3, 6.1-6.3, 6.5-6.6, 6.8-6.9).

public_methods.name: build
public_methods.signature: (self, experiment_name: str, n_paths_per_class: int, seed: int) -> tuple

tensor_flows.name: End-to-end experiment pipeline
steps: ExperimentBuilder resolves which simulators/classes/params the named experiment needs
Each simulator generates n_paths_per_class raw paths (Heston variance, OU, or rBergomi variance)
SignatureComputer.compute(paths, order, T) -> [n_paths, d_N] feature matrix per class
Concatenate + label -> (X, y); split into train/test (test=50,000/class as in the paper)
confusion_matrix(X_test, y_test) + accuracy computed and saved

heston.correlation_rho: -0.7  # ASSUMED: not given in paper, irrelevant since only v_t is used (confidence 0.3)

rbergomi.simulation_scheme: cholesky_fbm  # ASSUMED substitute for paper's GPU hybrid scheme (confidence 0.6); see docs/reproducibility_notes.md

runtime.reason: batched tensor ops for simulation + optional GPU signature/NN baseline
primary classifier, as specified in the paper
confusion matrix heatmaps, feature-importance bar charts

entrypoints.purpose: Run one named experiment end-to-end: simulate, extract signatures, train XGBoost, save model + results.

Classify a small batch of new/held-out paths using a saved model.

entrypoints.purpose: Paper-specific: reproduce Figure 6.4 (built-in vs permutation importance) for a trained experiment.

Output directory for the comparison plot

risk_assessment.description: The exact rough Bergomi simulation scheme (GPU-adapted hybrid scheme of Bennedsen et al. 2017) is not reproduced verbatim -- we substitute a Cholesky-based exact fBM simulation, which is mathematically equivalent in distribution but much slower (O(n^2)-O(n^3) vs. the hybrid scheme's near-linear cost) and may differ in fine discretization behavior at the 100-step resolution used.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Documented in README/reproducibility notes; for exact paper-scale reproduction (250k paths x multiple H values) a production system should swap in the actual hybrid-scheme implementation (e.g. adapting github.com/ryanmccrickerd/rough_bergomi as the paper itself did) rather than the Cholesky substitute shipped here, which is fine for the debug-scale smoke tests but too slow at full scale.

risk_assessment.description: GPU-vectorized signature computation is not reproduced verbatim (paper references an adapted third-party implementation); this repo uses the CPU-only `iisignature` package instead, which is correct but far slower at 250k-path scale.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Swap in the `signatory` PyTorch-native package (GPU-capable) for full-scale runs; iisignature is kept as the default for correctness-verification and small-scale/debug runs since it's simpler to install and has no GPU dependency.

risk_assessment.description: Heston discretization scheme and correlation rho are unstated; rho is argued to be irrelevant (only v_t is used) but the discretization scheme choice could shift fine details of the simulated variance path distribution.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Use a standard full-truncation Euler scheme (documented in code), consistent with the paper's Feller-condition-aware sampling of nu; flagged as tunable if results don't match.

risk_assessment.description: Neural-network baseline (Sec 6.7) hyperparameters (dropout, batch size, learning rate) are unstated.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Use literature-standard defaults (dropout 0.2, batch 256, Adam lr 1e-3); this is a one-off robustness check in the paper, not a primary result, so lower priority to get exactly right.
